does: This paper addresses hallucination in Vision-Language Models by introducing OSCAR, an online self-calibration framework that leverages the generative-discriminative gap within LVLMs—where models perform better at discriminative verification tasks than open-ended generation. The method combines Monte Carlo Tree Search with a Dual-Granularity Reward Mechanism to generate preference data without external supervision, then applies Direct Preference Optimization to iteratively refine the model. By grounding feedback in the model's own discriminative capabilities rather than relying on external supervisors, OSCAR achieves state-of-the-art performance on hallucination benchmarks while maintaining the model's perceptual alignment with actual image content.
connects: This work builds on recent preference alignment methods for LVLMs that have increasingly adopted supervision from stronger models like GPT, but identifies a critical limitation: the Supervision-Perception Mismatch where students are forced to match fine-grained supervision beyond their perceptual capacity. It extends prior research on hallucination in vision-language models and connects to broader work on self-supervised learning and preference optimization techniques. The paper also draws on the established generative-discriminative framework from machine learning, applying this classical insight to the specific challenge of LVLM calibration and building on Direct Preference Optimization as an efficient training method.
enables: This approach opens pathways for more robust self-improvement mechanisms in multimodal models without dependence on external model supervision. The framework could enable LVLMs to continuously refine themselves through internal verification capabilities, potentially reducing computational costs of alignment and scaling to more diverse vision-language tasks. The identified generative-discriminative gap suggests opportunities for developing hybrid architectures that better exploit discriminative strengths during training. Future work could extend this self-calibration paradigm to other hallucination types, apply it to larger model families, and investigate whether similar internal capability gaps exist across other modalities or generation tasks.
